title: v2 API Integration Tests — Patterns and Findings
summary: Go integration testing for workitemsv2: real-DB patterns, SSE streaming runner, 35 tests, Dev UI panel.
content:
Contents
  1. 1. Overview
  2. 2. Key Findings
  3. 3. Test Architecture
  4. 4. Real Database vs Mocks
  5. 5. Handler Testing with Chi
  6. 6. SSE Streaming Tests
  7. 7. Cleanup and Isolation
  8. 8. Run Patterns
  9. 9. Sources
  10. 10. Gaps

1. Overview

The MMFFDev Vector project introduced a v2 work-items API (PLA-0023) backed by the vector_artefacts PostgreSQL database. This research paper documents the integration-testing approach adopted for backend/internal/workitemsv2/: two test files (service_test.go, handler_test.go) covering 35 tests, a live-streaming Dev UI panel, and the SSE route that bridges Go test output to the browser. It also synthesises external best-practice research gathered to validate design decisions.

2. Key Findings

  • Real DB is correct for SQL-heavy code. Dialect differences (e.g. FOR UPDATE OF, ambiguous column references) are only caught against a live PostgreSQL instance — mocks would have silently passed the bug found in the BulkOps query.
  • Tunnel-skip pattern avoids CI noise. Tests call t.Skip() when the tunnel is down, so they never fail in environments without DB access.
  • External test package enforces public surface. Using package workitemsv2_test prevents tests from coupling to unexported internals.
  • t.Cleanup() is the correct teardown primitive. LIFO order and parallel-safe semantics make it superior to deferred deletes in subtests.
  • Nil-pool fallback tests need no DB. Nine handler tests and one service test exercise graceful degradation without any infrastructure dependency.
  • SSE streaming from go test -v works reliably via ReadableStream. Each stdout/stderr line is emitted as an SSE data: event; terminal events carry exit codes.
  • Dev UI panel provides clickable per-test execution. Each of the 35 tests is individually runnable from the browser with live-streamed output below the row.

3. Test Architecture

File layout

FilePackageTest countDB required
service_test.goworkitemsv2_test22Yes (most)
handler_test.goworkitemsv2_test134 of 13

Pool helpers

vaPool(t) and mainPool(t) load DSNs from backend/.env.dev and backend/.env.local, connect to PostgreSQL, and call t.Skip() on failure. The vector_artefacts DSN is derived by replacing the dbname in the mmff_vector DSN — the env var VECTOR_ARTEFACTS_DB_URL is not populated in .env.dev.

Seed helper

seedArtefact(t, va, sub, itemType, title) inserts a raw row into artefacts, infers workspace_id from an existing row in the same subscription (the column is NOT NULL), and registers a t.Cleanup DELETE. This ensures each test is hermetic.

Subscription fixture

Tests prefer subscription UUID 4dbcef71-f9d2-48e5-b19c-1bafc1767c67, which has all four work artefact types (Epic, Story, Task, Defect). A fallback scans for any subscription with ≥4 work types.

4. Real Database vs Mocks

The decision to test against a live PostgreSQL instance rather than mocks was validated by a concrete defect found during the test-writing phase: the BulkOps query contained SELECT id::text in a join between artefacts a and artefact_types at. PostgreSQL raised SQLSTATE 42702: column reference "id" is ambiguous. A mock would not have caught this.

External research (Three Dots Labs, 2024) confirms: "Testing against a real database is more accurate but requires infrastructure. SQL dialect differences mean the actual target database is more reliable than test doubles."

The fix was: qualify as a.id::text and add OF a to the FOR UPDATE clause. Both TestBulkOps_CrossTenantRejected and TestHandler_Bulk_SetPriority now pass.

5. Handler Testing with Chi

Handler tests use net/http/httptest with a minimal chi router. The helper newTestRouter(h, user) mounts all v2 routes and injects a test *models.User via auth.WithUserForTest(ctx, user).

Nine nil-pool tests run entirely in-process with no network or DB dependency, verifying:

  • 400 for invalid UUIDs and missing request bodies
  • 404 for not-found items when pool is nil
  • 200 with empty payload for list/summary/flow-states when pool is nil
  • Correct Content-Type: application/json on all responses

6. SSE Streaming Tests

The Dev UI uses a Next.js App Router API route (app/api/dev/go-test/route.ts) that spawns go test -v -count=1 <pkg> [-run <name>] and streams each stdout/stderr line as an SSE data: event. A terminal event: done or event: fail event carries the exit code.

SSE format

data: === RUN   TestListWorkItems_ReturnsTenantRows

data: --- PASS: TestListWorkItems_ReturnsTenantRows (0.12s)

event: done
data: exit 0

Security

The route validates pkg against /^\.\/[a-zA-Z0-9_./-]+$/ and run against /^[a-zA-Z0-9_]+$/ before spawning. The route returns 404 in production (NODE_ENV !== "development").

Browser-side consumption

The React panel uses fetch() with a ReadableStream reader. Each chunk is decoded, split on , and parsed for event: / data: lines. Terminal events flip the row status to pass (green) or fail (red). External research (thoughtbot, Pascal Allen) confirms this is the canonical pattern for SSE in the browser.

7. Cleanup and Isolation

Go 1.22+ resolves the loop-variable capture issue — tt := tt is no longer required for parallel subtests. The workitemsv2 tests do not use t.Parallel() because they share a single subscription fixture and sequential execution is fast enough (<2 s total).

t.Cleanup() executes in LIFO order, which is correct for multi-step seeding: if a test seeds parent then child, the child is deleted before the parent. This avoids FK constraint failures on cleanup.

8. Run Patterns

CommandEffect
BACKEND_ENV=dev go test -v -count=1 ./internal/workitemsv2/...Run all 35 tests against live DB
go test -v -count=1 -run TestNilPool ./internal/workitemsv2/...Run nil-pool tests only (no DB)
go test -v -count=1 -run TestHandler ./internal/workitemsv2/...Run handler tests only
Dev → API v2 Tests tab → click test rowStream a single test live in browser
Dev → API v2 Tests tab → Run allRun all 35 sequentially in browser

9. Sources

  1. Testing REST APIs in Go — DEV Community
  2. 4 Practical Principles of Database Integration Testing — Three Dots Labs
  3. Running Database Integration Tests in Go (2024) — chrisguitarguy.com
  4. Writing a Server Sent Events server in Go — thoughtbot
  5. Streaming Server-Sent Events With Go — Pascal Allen
  6. Live website updates with Go, SSE, and htmx — Three Dots Labs
  7. Using Subtests and Sub-benchmarks — The Go Blog
  8. TableDrivenTests — Go Wiki
  9. Table Driven Testing In Parallel — Gopher Guides
  10. Best Practices for REST API Testing in 2024 — Code Intelligence

10. Gaps

  • No parallel test execution explored — sequential is currently fast enough; revisit if suite grows beyond ~100 tests.
  • No load or fuzz testing of the v2 API endpoints.
  • CI integration (running tests in GitHub Actions against a real PostgreSQL service container) not yet configured.
  • VECTOR_ARTEFACTS_DB_URL derivation from mmff_vector DSN is a workaround; a first-class env var would be cleaner.
